---
title: "Introduction to Reproducibility in R"
author: "Andrew Brown and Mike Schmidt"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, style.scss]
editor: visual
# bibliography: references.bib
execute:
  echo: true
  error: true
---

## Overview

!! Fill out when done

## Reproducibility: Why

Programming, in part, helps us repeat repetitive tasks efficiently and in an explainable, repeatable way.

**Reproducibility** is a tool to those ends, particularly towards the explainable and repeatable goals.

Can someone understand and repeat what I've done?

## Reproducibility: What

1. Reproducibility is: Writing scripts in a way where they are usable by more than one person.
2. Reproducibility is: Writing scripts where they will work across systems (etc. R version, OS, package versions, etc.)
3. Reproducibility is: Writing scripts so that they are data source and file path agnostic! (`setwd()` no more)

## An introduction to Reproducibility: File Paths

To begin our journey towards reproduciblity let's first discuss file paths.

- If you open a standalone script, most IDEs (Rstudio and VS Code) start relative to %USERPROFILE% root. On a Windows machine that would be "C://Users/username".
- To make an R session “relative” to the directory you are working in most of us first learn to use `setwd()`.
- Even though it is convenient, a good step towards reproducibility is to stop using `setwd()`.

## An introduction to Reproducibility: Why no `setwd()`

- Reproducibility at its core is making your programs run without editing.
- So if you share a script and another person has to change `setwd("/c/Users/michaelschmidt/proj/folder/loc")` to `setwd("/c/Users/andrewbrown/different/proj/folder/location")`.
- While this is small it is a good first step towards reproducibility.

## But how do I not use `setwd()`

There are a few options:

- The `{here}` package has the `here()` function to create file paths as well as a helper file, `.here` to set the base of a project (this inconsistently works for me). `{here}` can also be helpful in understanding where you are in a project.
- My preferred choice is to use and generate `.Rproject`s in Rstudio with the GUI or from the command line with `usethis::create_project()`

## Rstudio Setup with RProjects: Global Options

Under Tools>Global Options: In the General tab

![](images/rdata_rhistory_settings.png)

This insures that every R session on startup is fresh with no history to mess up any given script or project.

## Rprojects

To create a project go to File>New Project.
![](images/new_project_options.png)

## Rprojects

Select Rproject
![](images/new_project_options2.png)

## Rprojects

Browse to select a parent folder and project folder name then select Create Project.
![](images/new_project_options3.png)

## Rprojects

The project should open in Rstudio and you should see an empty project with an .Rproj file.  
![](images/project_file.png)

## R projects

If you click on that file you will have an options window that has options similar to the Global options:
![](images/project_options_window.png)

## Rprojects

But more importantly your R session is now relative to your Rproject parent folder, and you never need to `setwd()` again so long as your project is active.
![](images/getwd_project.png)

## Rprojects

If you are no longer working in the project and want to initialize it in Rstudio go to File>Open Project or File>Recent Projects or navigate in explorer to the .Rproj file a double click it, that should open the project in Rstudio.
You will know you are working in a .Rproject when you see it set in the upper right corn of your Rstudio session.

![](images/rproj_icon.png)

## Rprojects

- Now that we've created an Rproject, and it is active, we can create and write scripts as we otherwise would.
- Important note: if the project is active, and you are working on a script in a folder outside the project folder, R will still be relative to the active project's root.

## Rproject: Renv

Now that we have relative file paths, and we know the next user of our scripts.

a. Git
b. Renv

## Packages

a. maybe outside the scope but definitely should be mentioned as a tool for reproducibility

## Docker

## Targets

## Examples

a. R targets pipeline
